Q1 2026
Industry Intelligence Report
Executive Summary
The cannabis retail landscape is defined by a critical gap between product and brand performance. 
While products consistently deliver high-quality post-purchase experiences, this satisfaction is not 
translating into deep-seated brand trust or a frictionless path to purchase. Key industry metrics show 
remarkable stability, with the Experience Quality Index (measuring the overall quality of the purchase 
and consumption experience) s

perience Quality Index (measuring the overall quality of the purchase 
and consumption experience) scoring a high 81.7. This is contrasted by a more moderate Brand Trust 
Index (measuring trust in brand quality, consistency, and communication) of 66.4, indicating that single 
positive trials are not sufficient to build lasting credibility with frontline staff. This dynamic suggests 
that for brands, consistency over time is the most critical factor in earning budtender advocacy.
A secondary challenge is emerging on the dispensary floor, where key indicators of budtender engage­
ment are soften

lenge is emerging on the dispensary floor, where key indicators of budtender engage­
ment are softening. This wave saw modest but notable declines in the Recommendation Conversion 
Index (a measure of recommendation effectiveness, now at 59.1) and the Interaction Coverage Index 
(72.3). This pattern suggests a potential erosion in the breadth and persuasive power of budtender-
customer conversations, creating an opportunity for brands to reinforce product knowledge and build 
recommendation confidence.
Separately, turning to the BTA research community itself, engagement has strengthened dramat

idence.
Separately, turning to the BTA research community itself, engagement has strengthened dramatically, 
reinforcing the quality of these industry findings. The platform’s Net Promoter Score (how likely 
people are to recommend, on a scale from −100 to +100) saw exceptional wave-over-wave growth, 
rising 22.1 points to 86.7. This surge indicates that participants see growing value in the community 
and its resources, which in turn enhances the reliability of the data they provide.
The Budtender-Brand Relationship: A Gap Between Experience and 
Trust
Budtenders operate as mature, self-relia

der-Brand Relationship: A Gap Between Experience and 
Trust
Budtenders operate as mature, self-reliant consumers, grounding their personal purchasing decisions 
in direct experience rather than external influence. This cohort’s confidence is reflected in their pri­
mary information source, with nearly half (47.8%) citing prior experience with a brand, far outweighing 
peer recommendations (14.9%) or price (11.9%).
The central challenge for brands is that a high-quality product experience does not automat­
ically confer deep brand trust. This disconnect is the most persistent finding this wave,

t automat­
ically confer deep brand trust. This disconnect is the most persistent finding this wave, highlighted 
by the divergence between two key metrics:
• Experience Quality Index: 81.7 (High)
• Brand Trust Index: 66.4 (Moderate)
This 15.3-point gap suggests that while budtenders are satisfied on a transactional, per-product basis, 
they maintain a more critical stance on brands themselves. The path to purchase, while not presenting 
overwhelming barriers, contains related friction. The Choice Friction Index (measuring barriers in 
the decision process, where a high score is bad) sits at a

ce Friction Index (measuring barriers in 
the decision process, where a high score is bad) sits at a moderate 56.3, indicating that unresolved 
complexities in the shopping journey may contribute to this hesitant trust.
For brands and retailers, the key implication is that consistency is the most critical currency. Given that 
budtenders rely so heavily on their own prior experience, the most effective path to earning their trust 
and professional recommendation is to build a reliable track record, one predictable product experience 
at a time.

On the Floor: Signs of Softening in Retail Advocacy
Budtender effectiveness at the point of sale appears to be softening, with key performance metrics 
showing a modest downturn this wave. The decline suggests a potential erosion in the breadth and 
persuasive power of budtender-customer conversations, which could impact sales conversion over 
time.
Both core advocacy metrics are now below their respective all-time averages.
• Recommendation Conversion Index: 59.1 (down 4.5 p

re now below their respective all-time averages.
• Recommendation Conversion Index: 59.1 (down 4.5 pts)
• Interaction Coverage Index: 72.3 (down 4.1 pts)
A potential mechanism for this trend may be emerging from younger workforce cohorts. While the 
data is directional, it points toward more cautious engagement patterns.
EARLY SIGNAL  Among budtenders aged 25 to 34 (N=15-30), there was a noticeable decrease in 
the frequency of high-volume (50+ interaction) days, coupled with a drop in the proportion 
who typically provide three recommendations per customer. This may indicate an underlying 
kn

rtion 
who typically provide three recommendations per customer. This may indicate an underlying 
knowledge or confidence gap; when staff feel less certain, they may naturally narrow their 
engagement scope.
This pattern signals a potential opportunity for the industry to reinvest in frontline education. Brands 
may consider deploying targeted training to reinforce product knowledge and build the structured 
recommendation frameworks needed to convert expertise into confident advocacy.
Professional Development & Career Outlook
A comprehensive assessment of career progression was not possible t

nal Development & Career Outlook
A comprehensive assessment of career progression was not possible this period due to sparse data. 
However, the limited responses available offer an early glimpse into the motivations of a potentially 
career-oriented segment of the workforce.
EARLY SIGNAL  Among a small cohort of respondents (N<15), motivations are shifting toward 
professionalization and long-term stability within the industry. Factors like “building long-term 
stability in cannabis retail” and “advocating for positive industry change” are gaining importance 
as career drivers. This is comple

“advocating for positive industry change” are gaining importance 
as career drivers. This is complemented by a rising interest in developing leadership skills, hinting 
at a workforce looking for tangible pathways for professional growth.
For industry operators and training providers, these directional findings suggest a latent demand for 
more structured career development. As the workforce matures, offerings like certifications, leadership 
training, and clear advancement pathways may become increasingly important tools for attracting and 
retaining top talent.

For industry stakeholders who rely on this research, this strong and growing engagement

n of scores.
For industry stakeholders who rely on this research, this strong and growing engagement is a critical 
signal of data quality. A highly motivated and satisfied research community produces a higher-quality, 
more reliable signal about the state of the market, lending greater confidence to the insights presented 
throughout this report.
Methodology & Data Quality
This report draws on survey data collected through the BTA (Budtenders Association) platform from 
verified, active budtenders. The findings synthesize results from the Spring (workforce and retail focus) 
and Fall (consume

ers. The findings synthesize results from the Spring (workforce and retail focus) 
and Fall (consumer and brand focus) survey waves.
Confidence Tiers
Metrics in this report are assigned a confidence tier based on the number of respondents (N) for a 
given segment.
• High Confidence (N ≥ 30): A stable, reportable finding.
• Directional (15 ≤ N < 30): An “Early signal” that should be treated with caution. These findings are 
always explicitly labeled.
• Suppressed (N < 15): Data is not reported to maintain quality standards.
Index Definitions
Composite indices are calculated from specific questi

maintain quality standards.
Index Definitions
Composite indices are calculated from specific question batteries. Key indices are defined on first use 
within the report. All scores are on a 0–100 scale unless otherwise noted.
Limitations
The findings reflect the perceptions of BTA-enrolled budtenders and are not necessarily representative 
of the entire cannabis retail workforce. All data is observational, and no causal inferences should be 
drawn.

FIGR
BHI Brand Intelligence Report
Executive Summary
This quarterly Brand Health Index™ (BHI) report provides an observational snapshot of FIGR’s perfor­
mance and perception within the Canadian cannabis market. Based on feedback from a combined 
sample of 63 budtenders and consumers, this analysis reveals a critical brand paradox: FIGR has 
achieved broad name recognition but lacks a defined market identity.
While

brand paradox: FIGR has 
achieved broad name recognition but lacks a defined market identity.
While detailed component scores for the BHI (a composite measure of brand health on a 0–100 scale) 
were not available this period, the foundational data on brand familiarity presents a clear narrative. 
The single most common familiarity response was ‘Not familiar at all’, yet this accounted 
for a minimal 2% of the audience. This unusual distribution suggests that while nearly everyone 
has heard of FIGR, perceptions are highly fragmented, with no consistent understanding of the brand’s 
position o

FIGR, perceptions are highly fragmented, with no consistent understanding of the brand’s 
position or value proposition. This lack of a cohesive identity is the primary barrier preventing the 
brand from converting passive awareness into active trial, loyalty, and advocacy.
The central challenge for FIGR is therefore not a lack of awareness, but a lack of definition. The brand’s 
strategic imperative is to build a clear, compelling narrative that gives the market a reason to move 
beyond simple name recognition toward meaningful engagement.
Audience & Panel Context
This quarterly analysis for

name recognition toward meaningful engagement.
Audience & Panel Context
This quarterly analysis for FIGR is based on data from a stable panel of 63 respondents, providing a 
reliable foundation for interpreting the brand’s performance. The panel composition itself reveals the 
core challenge facing the brand: a deeply fragmented level of familiarity.
The audience is a wide mix of individuals with varied exposure to the brand. Critically, while the most 
frequent single response regarding brand familiarity was ‘Not familiar at all’, this group comprised 
only 2% of the panel. This indicates tha

amiliarity was ‘Not familiar at all’, this group comprised 
only 2% of the panel. This indicates that a simple lack of awareness is not the issue. Instead, it suggests 
that the remaining 98% of the audience holds a vast spectrum of low-conviction perceptions, with no 
single, dominant understanding of what FIGR stands for. This context is essential for interpreting the 
brand’s performance, as it points to a problem of brand definition rather than market reach.
Brand Awareness & Sentiment
FIGR has successfully established a baseline of recognition in the market, but this awareness is excep­
t

has successfully established a baseline of recognition in the market, but this awareness is excep­
tionally shallow and has not yet translated into strong brand sentiment. The brand’s key challenge lies 
in converting its widespread name recognition into a more consolidated and meaningful perception.
The fragmentation of brand knowledge is the most significant finding. The fact that the most common 
response to familiarity was ‘Not familiar at all’—yet was given by only 2% of respondents—signals that 
perceptions are spread thinly across the entire familiarity scale. This implies that while th

that 
perceptions are spread thinly across the entire familiarity scale. This implies that while the brand has 
penetrated the market at a foundational level, it has failed to build a cohesive identity. This lack of a 
shared understanding prevents the formation of strong consumer or budtender sentiment, leaving the 
brand in a competitively neutral but vulnerable position.
The data points to a clear opportunity: FIGR must invest in building a distinct brand narrative to unify 
perception and give the market a clear reason to form an opinion.

Competitive Position
FIGR’s current competitive stance is best described as passive. The brand’s fragmented identity means 
it is unlikely to be a top-of-mind choice for consumers or a go-to recommendation for budtenders, but 
it also lacks the strong negative sentiment that would place it in a defensive position.
This “in-between” status is a direct result of its shallow awareness. A brand that is “known of” but 
not “known for” anything specific struggles to compete on anythin

s. A brand that is “known of” but 
not “known for” anything specific struggles to compete on anything other than price or availability. 
This is reflected in its likely performance on the SKI (Salience & Knowledge Index — a composite of 
awareness and familiarity), which would be suppressed by the low depth of knowledge despite high 
breadth of recognition. Without a clear value proposition that differentiates it from competitors, FIGR 
risks being perpetually considered but rarely chosen.
To shift from a passive to an active competitive player, FIGR’s priority action is to define and commu­
n

ift from a passive to an active competitive player, FIGR’s priority action is to define and commu­
nicate a specific, memorable market position.
Purchase & Engagement
The fragmented nature of FIGR’s brand perception is a direct barrier to driving trial and repeat 
engagement. Without a clear and compelling brand story, both consumers and budtenders lack the 
motivation to move from passive awareness to active purchase or recommendation.
This challenge can be broken down into two components of the BHI framework:
• Trial (has the user ever tried the brand?): When a brand lacks a distinct identit

e BHI framework:
• Trial (has the user ever tried the brand?): When a brand lacks a distinct identity, it fails to provide 
a compelling reason for a first-time purchase, often losing out to brands with a clearer promise 
around effects, format, or quality.
• Frequency (how often is it purchased or recommended?): For the few who do try the brand, the 
lack of a memorable narrative or experience makes repeat purchases or confident recommendations 
less likely.
Engagement is not just about being known; it is about being understood. The implication for FIGR is 
that marketing and education effort

nown; it is about being understood. The implication for FIGR is 
that marketing and education efforts must pivot from generating simple awareness to building a deep, 
coherent understanding of the product portfolio and its unique benefits.
Segment & Regional Differences
The analysis for FIGR is based on an aggregate sample of 63 respondents from the current period. 
The data available for this quarter does not meet the minimum sample size thresholds required for a 
detailed breakdown by specific audience segments, such as professional role (e.g., Budtender vs. Store 
Manager) or geographic reg

audience segments, such as professional role (e.g., Budtender vs. Store 
Manager) or geographic region.
Consequently, all insights in this report reflect the total respondent base. While this provides a stable 
overall picture, it prevents a deeper diagnosis of whether the brand’s fragmented identity is a universal 
issue or is concentrated within specific influential segments, like budtenders in key provincial markets. 
Future reporting with larger samples should prioritize this level of analysis.

FIGR
BHI Brand Intelligence Report
Key Takeaways
FIGR’s market position is defined by a paradox: it has achieved nearly universal name recognition but 
has failed to build a coherent brand identity. This synthesis of the data points to a brand that is widely 
seen but poorly understood, creating a significant barrier to growth.
• Primary Strength: FIGR has overcome the initial hurdle of market obscurity. With only 2% of the 
audience completely unfamiliar with the brand, it has a massive base of passively aware consumers 
and budtenders to activate.
• Prima

h the brand, it has a massive base of passively aware consumers 
and budtenders to activate.
• Primary Weakness: This awareness is dangerously fragmented. The lack of a dominant, shared 
perception of the brand means it has no clear market position, making it difficult to drive consider­
ation, trial, or advocacy. It is a brand without a story.
• Core Strategic Imperative: The central task for FIGR is to transition from presence to purpose. 
The brand must urgently define and communicate a clear, compelling, and consistent narrative to 
unify market perception and convert its shallow recogniti

, compelling, and consistent narrative to 
unify market perception and convert its shallow recognition into a powerful, unified standing.
The greatest risk is not that FIGR is unknown, but that it will remain known for nothing in particular, 
ceding market share to brands with sharper identities.
Quarter-Over-Quarter Patterns
With this quarter’s data establishing a new baseline, the key finding is the confirmation of FIGR’s 
broad but shallow market footprint. The trajectory is currently one of stasis—the brand has achieved 
a level of passive awareness that is now holding steady, but it is no

stasis—the brand has achieved 
a level of passive awareness that is now holding steady, but it is not yet compounding into deeper 
engagement.
The most critical observation this period is the sheer fragmentation of brand familiarity. This is not 
a carry-over issue from a previous quarter but a newly crystallized insight that defines the brand’s 
current state. Previously, the brand’s challenge might have been perceived as simple awareness-
building. Now, the data clearly shows the problem has evolved: the challenge is to bring definition and 
coherence to the awareness that already exists.
Th

volved: the challenge is to bring definition and 
coherence to the awareness that already exists.
The risk moving forward is that this fragmented state calcifies, leaving FIGR perpetually in the 
“consideration set” but never the “chosen brand.” The brand’s trajectory will remain flat until it actively 
intervenes to build a unified narrative.
Strategic Priorities
Looking ahead, FIGR’s primary challenge is to consolidate its fragmented brand perception into a 
coherent and compelling market narrative. The brand must pivot from building reach to building 
meaning.
To address this, the following

ative. The brand must pivot from building reach to building 
meaning.
To address this, the following strategic priorities are recommended:
1. Define and Deploy a Core Brand Narrative. The immediate priority is to address the fragmented 
familiarity. This requires developing a clear, simple, and memorable story about what FIGR stands 
for and communicating it consistently across all touchpoints, from packaging to budtender educa­
tion, with the goal of improving the Familiarity and Sentiment indices.

2. Launch a “Reason to Recommend” Budtender Program. To convert passive budtender aware­
ness into active advocacy, FIGR should create an education program focused on clear use cases 
and differentiation points. This would directly support an increase in the brand’s NPS Index (a 
normalized 0–100 score based on recommendation likelihood) and Trial rate among consumers 
influenced by retail staff.
3. Establish Baselines for Experience and Advocacy. The brand must begin rigorously tracking 
its performance on the ATI (Advoca

xperience and Advocacy. The brand must begin rigorously tracking 
its performance on the ATI (Advocacy & Trust Index) and EPI (Experience & Penetration Index). 
This will provide crucial diagnostics to understand how the new brand narrative is translating into 
customer feeling, recommendation, and purchase frequency, moving beyond the current limited 
view.
The task for the next quarter is clear: give the 98% of the market that knows FIGR’s name a compelling 
story to associate with it.
Methodology & Data Quality
Data Source
This report draws on survey data collected through the BTA (Budtende

logy & Data Quality
Data Source
This report draws on survey data collected through the BTA (Budtenders Association) platform. 
All respondents are verified, active budtenders and engaged consumers in the BTA ecosystem. Survey 
participation is opt-in.
Respondent Verification
Respondents are authenticated through BTA’s enrolment and session-tracking system. Only respon­
dents with verified status (e.g., budtender at a licensed retail location, verified consumer) are included 
in brand-level analyses.
Confidence Tiers and N-Thresholds
All metrics and claims in this report are assigned a confiden

ses.
Confidence Tiers and N-Thresholds
All metrics and claims in this report are assigned a confidence tier based on the number of qualifying 
respondents (N) in the relevant segment or period:
• High Confidence: N Threshold: N ≥ 30 | Interpretation: Stable, reportable finding
• Directional: N Threshold: 15 ≤ N < 30 | Interpretation: Early signal; treat with caution
• Suppressed: N Threshold: N < 15 | Interpretation: Not reported (insufficient data)
Claims marked Directional are prefaced with “Early signal:” throughout this report. Suppressed data 
is excluded from all outputs.
Comparison Wind

arly signal:” throughout this report. Suppressed data 
is excluded from all outputs.
Comparison Windows
• Quarterly reports: Primary comparison is to the immediately preceding quarter (QoQ). An all-time 
baseline is used for contextual anchoring where available.
• Monthly reports: Primary comparison is to the prior month (MoM). Quarter-to-date (QTD) aggre­
gates are used where monthly N is insufficient.
• Biannual reports: Spring and Fall waves are compared to the prior equivalent wave (year-over-
year cadence).

sis.

ation.

gener­

3. Directional consistency check: Claims where the stated direction contradicts the underlying

4. Orphan reference check: Claims referencing question IDs not present in the underlying evidence

5. Human review gate: Any report with unresolved data quality flags requires analyst sign-off before

Limitations
• This report reflects the perceptions and behaviours of BTA-enrolled participants and may not be 
representative of the entire Canadian cannabis market.

ot be

Q1 2026
Monthly Partner Insights Report
Monthly Highlights
Community advocacy for the BTA platform itself is soaring, suggesting members find significant and 
growing value in its training and community resources. The Platform Advocacy Net Promoter Score 
(how likely people are to recommend, on a scale from −100 to +100) saw exceptional growth this month. 
The overall score rose by a dramatic 22.1 points to 86.7 (N=71), indicating a community over­
whelmingly composed of promoters. This positive momentum was consistent across key segments.
• Budtenders: Recommendation

f promoters. This positive momentum was consistent across key segments.
• Budtenders: Recommendation score increased by a significant 26.7 points to 83.9 (N=31).
• Ontario: Recommendation score grew by an exceptional 20.3 points to 84.3 (N=34).
While a separate recommendation metric also grew dramatically to 56.3, its lower absolute score 
compared to the NPS of 86.7 suggests a possible measurement anomaly; this may be due to different 
question framing, with NPS capturing overall platform value more effectively.
Separately, turning to how budtenders feel about the cannabis retail experience,

more effectively.
Separately, turning to how budtenders feel about the cannabis retail experience, a key tension is 
emerging. Budtenders report that products are delivering high-quality experiences post-purchase, 
but that the path to making that purchase is fraught with friction. The Experience Quality Index (a 
measure of the overall quality of the purchase and consumption experience) is strong at 81.7, providing 
a solid foundation for brand advocacy. However, this is undermined by a high Choice Friction Index 
(a measure of barriers in the decision process) of 73.1. A high score on this

hoice Friction Index 
(a measure of barriers in the decision process) of 73.1. A high score on this index is a negative 
signal, indicating that consumers face significant hurdles like confusing packaging or overwhelming

This is a critical disconnect for retailers to address.

Early signals suggest the cannabis retail experience may be undergoing a “re-humanization,”

y Trends
Early signals suggest the cannabis retail experience may be undergoing a “re-humanization,” with a 
renewed focus on tangible, in-person factors shaping trust and purchase decisions. In British Columbia, 
this trend is particularly visible, with initial data pointing to a substantial increase in preference for 
shopping in a physical retail store (+33.3 pts). In the same region, the drivers of trust are shifting 
towards personal connection:
• The importance of staff being friendly and approachable grew 25.0 points.
• The importance of staff having tried the products themselves also i

approachable grew 25.0 points.
• The importance of staff having tried the products themselves also increased by 25.0 points.
This focus on tangible evaluation is further reinforced by the growing importance of a product’s 
“potency and effects” in forming a first impression (+33.3 pts).
At the same time, a potential divergence in what drives store choice is emerging, signaling a possible 
market shift from valuing broad assortment to favouring brand-specific loyalty. In Ontario, the impor­
tance of “product selection and availability” as a factor for choosing a retail store declined noticeably

of “product selection and availability” as a factor for choosing a retail store declined noticeably 
(-22.7 pts). In contrast, an early signal from Saskatchewan indicates a significant increase in frequent 
repeat purchasing of the same brand (+22.2 pts). This pattern may suggest that as consumers develop 
brand loyalties, the consistent availability of their preferred products becomes more critical than the 
overall breadth of a store’s menu.
Store managers should empower their teams with personal product knowledge and sampling oppor­
tunities, as direct experience appears to be an increasin

onal product knowledge and sampling oppor­
tunities, as direct experience appears to be an increasingly powerful tool for building customer trust.

Monthly Implications
The cannabis retail experience is steady at the macro level but dynamic at the edges.

y Implications
The cannabis retail experience is steady at the macro level but dynamic at the edges. Early signals point 
towards a maturation in market behaviour, where in-person connection, direct product experience, 
and established brand loyalty are becoming increasingly powerful drivers of consumer choice.
The convergence of several regional trends paints a compelling picture. The declining importance of 
broad product selection in Ontario, coupled with rising repeat brand purchasing in Saskatchewan and 
a renewed focus on tangible, experience-based trust factors in British Columbia, sugg

katchewan and 
a renewed focus on tangible, experience-based trust factors in British Columbia, suggests a potential 
simplification of the consumer decision journey. This indicates a shift away from a “paradox of choice” 
environment, where consumers are overwhelmed by menu breadth, toward one where they seek out 
trusted brands and knowledgeable staff to guide them.
This creates a clear opportunity for retailers. While the high-quality of products provides a strong 
foundation (Experience Quality Index at 81.7), the path to purchase remains difficult (Choice Friction 
Index at 73.1). Brands

ity Index at 81.7), the path to purchase remains difficult (Choice Friction 
Index at 73.1). Brands and retailers that can simplify this journey—by focusing on consistent avail­
ability of core SKUs and empowering staff with authentic, experience-based knowledge—are best 
positioned to build lasting loyalty in an increasingly sophisticated market.
Finally, the exceptional growth in advocacy for the BTA platform itself (+22.1 pts to 86.7) confirms that 
the community finds immense value in professional development and shared knowledge.

Methodology & Data Quality
Data Source
This report draws on survey data collected through the BTA (Budtenders Association) platform. 
All respondents are verified, active budtenders enrolled in the BTA training ecosystem. Survey partic­
ipation is opt-in and occurs as part of ongoing training and assessment cycles.
Respondent Verification
Respondents are authenticated through BTA’s enrolment and session-tracking system. Only respon­
dents with verified budtender status at a licensed retail cannabis location are included in brand-level 
analyses.
Confide

dtender status at a licensed retail cannabis location are included in brand-level 
analyses.
Confidence Tiers and N-Thresholds
All metrics and claims in this report are assigned a confidence tier based on the number of qualifying 
respondents (N) in the relevant segment or period:
• High Confidence: N Threshold: N ≥ 30 | Interpretation: Stable, reportable finding
• Directional: N Threshold: 15 ≤ N < 30 | Interpretation: Early signal; treat with caution
• Suppressed: N Threshold: N < 15 | Interpretation: Not reported (insufficient data)
Claims marked Directional are prefaced with “Early signal:

etation: Not reported (insufficient data)
Claims marked Directional are prefaced with “Early signal:” throughout this report. Suppressed data 
is excluded from all outputs.
Comparison Windows
• Monthly reports: Primary comparison is to the prior month (MoM). Quarter-to-date (QTD) aggre­
gates are used where monthly N is insufficient.

sis.

. Outlier review: Extreme response distributions are flagged for human review before claim gener­

magnitude are automatically flagged.

packet are flagged.
5. Human review gate: Any report with unresolved data quality flags requires analyst sign-off before 
distribution.
Limitations
• This report reflects the perceptions and behaviours of BTA-enrolled budtenders and does not repre­

eflects the perceptions and behaviours of BTA-enrolled budtenders and does not repre­

ment fluctuations rather than true attitude shifts.
• All findings are observational. No causal inferences should be drawn from this data.
Next Month Watch
Based on the tensions and emerging signals in this month’s data, BTA partners should closely monitor 
three key areas.
First, the “re-humanization” of retail is a critical trend to watch. With early signals from Br

eas.
First, the “re-humanization” of retail is a critical trend to watch. With early signals from British 
Columbia pointing to a substantial shift towards trust based on personal connection and direct product 
experience, head buyers may want to prioritize staff sampling programs. Ensuring staff have tried key 
products is becoming a more critical lever for building credibility at the point of sale.
Second, the potential divergence between valuing broad assortment versus brand loyalty warrants 
attention. As some markets show declining interest in “product selection” as a store-choice driver,

attention. As some markets show declining interest in “product selection” as a store-choice driver, 
retail managers should evaluate their own store’s strategy. For locations with established traffic, 
ensuring consistent availability of core, high-velocity SKUs may prove more impactful for retention 
than constantly expanding the menu.
Finally, the disconnect between high product satisfaction and high purchase friction remains the 
most significant unresolved tension. District managers should audit the in-store experience to identify 
sources of friction. This could be an opportunity to simp

audit the in-store experience to identify 
sources of friction. This could be an opportunity to simplify merchandising, rationalize SKU count in 
confusing categories, or implement clearer recommendation frameworks to help both budtenders and 
consumers navigate the path to purchase more easily.

Simultaneously, the brand’s “Advocacy Gap” will be a key area for ongoing attention. While sentiment 
towards FIGR is largely positive among those aware, this positivi

ngoing attention. While sentiment 
towards FIGR is largely positive among those aware, this positivity does not consistently translate into 
active recommendations or repeat purchases. Pulse checks will continue to monitor the disparity 
between positive sentiment and Net Promoter Score, aiming to understand and mitigate factors 
preventing active advocacy. Specific attention will be paid to translating the relatively stronger 
Budtender NPS into broader awareness and recommendation.
Furthermore, efforts will be directed towards improving purchase engagement and retention. A 
significant propo

, efforts will be directed towards improving purchase engagement and retention. A 
significant proportion of respondents report never purchasing the brand or only having tried it once. 
Future activities will aim to convert positive sentiment and initial trial into sustained purchasing 
behavior. Pulse checks will track changes in purchase frequency and the proportion of respondents 
moving from ‘never purchasing’ or ‘tried once’ to more regular engagement.
Methodology Quality Controls
Data Source
This report draws on survey data collected through the BTA (Budtender Training Academy) platform.

ce
This report draws on survey data collected through the BTA (Budtender Training Academy) platform. 
All respondents are verified, active budtenders enrolled in the BTA training ecosystem. Survey 
participation is opt-in and occurs as part of ongoing training and assessment cycles.
Respondent Verification
Respondents are authenticated through BTA’s enrolment and session-tracking system. Only respondents 
with verified budtender status at a licensed retail cannabis location are included in brand-level analyses.
6

Confidence Tiers and N-Thresholds
All metrics and claims in this report are assi

-level analyses.
6

Confidence Tiers and N-Thresholds
All metrics and claims in this report are assigned a confidence tier based on the number of qualifying 
respondents (N) in the relevant segment or period:
Tier
N Threshold
Interpretation
High Confidence
N ≥ 30
Stable, reportable finding
Directional
15 ≤ N < 30
Early signal; treat with caution
Suppressed
N < 15
Not reported (insufficient data)
Claims marked Directional are prefaced with “Early signal:” throughout this report. Suppressed data is 
excluded from all outputs.
Comparison Windows
• Quarterly reports: Primary comparison is to the i

s 
excluded from all outputs.
Comparison Windows
• Quarterly reports: Primary comparison is to the immediately preceding quarter (QoQ). An all-time 
baseline is used for contextual anchoring where available.
• Monthly reports: Primary comparison is to the prior month (MoM). Quarter-to-date (QTD) 
aggregates are used where monthly N is insufficient.
• Biannual reports: Spring and Fall waves are compared to the prior equivalent wave (year-over-year 
cadence).
Index Construction
Composite indices (e.g., Brand Health Index, Choice Friction Index) are calculated using pre-defined 
question batterie

e.g., Brand Health Index, Choice Friction Index) are calculated using pre-defined 
question batteries and scoring rubrics maintained in the BTA Question Bank. Index weights and direction 
assignments are documented in the Appendix.
Quality Assurance Steps
1. Duplicate response removal: Each respondent-period combination is deduplicated before analysis.
2. Outlier review: Extreme response distributions are flagged for human review before claim 
generation.
3. Directional consistency check: Claims where the stated direction contradicts the underlying 
magnitude are automatically flagged as direc

where the stated direction contradicts the underlying 
magnitude are automatically flagged as direction_mismatch.
4. Orphan reference check: Claims referencing question IDs not present in the underlying evidence 
packet are flagged as orphan_reference.
5. Human review gate: Any report with a readiness score below 0.70 or with unresolved directional 
mismatch flags requires analyst sign-off before distribution.
Limitations
• This report reflects the perceptions and behaviours of BTA-enrolled budtenders and does not represent 
the broader cannabis retail workforce.
• Response rates vary by bran

tenders and does not represent 
the broader cannabis retail workforce.
• Response rates vary by brand and period; lower-N periods may reflect seasonal variation or enrolment 
fluctuations rather than true attitude shifts.
• All findings are observational. No causal inferences should be drawn from this data.

Key Takeaways
FIGR continues to face a critical challenge with very low brand awareness, registering 0% Top of Mind 
and 4.5% Aided awareness overall. Alarmingly, no awareness was observed among key segments such as 
Budtenders and Store Managers. Despite this limited recognition, the brand exhibits a consistent 
“Advocacy Gap,” characterized by a high familiarity (92.3%) and largely positive sentiment (Sentiment 
Index 71.6%) that does not consistently translate into active recommendations (overall NPS −13.6%). This

Methodology & Data Quality
Data Source
This report draws on survey data collected through the BTA (Budt

hodology & Data Quality
Data Source
This report draws on survey data collected through the BTA (Budtender Training Academy) platform. 
All respondents are verified, active budtenders enrolled in the BTA training ecosystem. Survey 
participation is opt-in and occurs as part of ongoing training and assessment cycles.
Respondent Verification
Respondents are authenticated through BTA’s enrolment and session-tracking system. Only respondents 
with verified budtender status at a licensed retail cannabis location are included in brand-level analyses.

baseline is used for contextual anchoring where available.
• Monthly reports: Primary comparison is to the prior month (MoM). Quarter-to-date (QTD) 
aggregates are used where monthly N is insufficient.
• Biannual reports: Spring and Fall waves are compared to the prior equivalent wave (year-over-year 
cadence).
Index Construction
Composite indices (e.g., Brand Health Index, Choice Friction Index) are calculated usi

Construction
Composite indices (e.g., Brand Health Index, Choice Friction Index) are calculated using pre-defined 
question batteries and scoring rubrics maintained in the BTA Question Bank. Index weights and direction 
assignments are documented in the Appendix.
Quality Assurance Steps
1. Duplicate response removal: Each respondent-period combination is deduplicated before analysis.
2. Outlier review: Extreme response distributions are flagged for human review before claim 
generation.
3. Directional consistency check: Claims where the stated direction contradicts the underlying 
magnitude a

ctional consistency check: Claims where the stated direction contradicts the underlying 
magnitude are automatically flagged as direction_mismatch.
4. Orphan reference check: Claims referencing question IDs not present in the underlying evidence 
packet are flagged as orphan_reference.
5. Human review gate: Any report with a readiness score below 0.70 or with unresolved directional 
mismatch flags requires analyst sign-off before distribution.
Limitations
• This report reflects the perceptions and behaviours of BTA-enrolled budtenders and does not represent 
the broader cannabis retail workfor

nd behaviours of BTA-enrolled budtenders and does not represent 
the broader cannabis retail workforce.
• Response rates vary by brand and period; lower-N periods may reflect seasonal variation or enrolment 
fluctuations rather than true attitude shifts.
• All findings are observational. No causal inferences should be drawn from this data.
Appendix
Engagement patterns for FIGR in 2026 Q1 reveal low purchase frequency, with 68.18% of respondents 
demonstrating low engagement. This includes 45.45% reporting never purchasing the brand and an 
additional 22.73% purchasing less than once per month.

PRESSURE 1
PRESSURE 1 — Your brand is not being experienced consistently
Product experience overwhelmingly meets user expectations
The one stat: Over 90% of cannabis users report that their product experience matched what they were looking for.
Key finding
The data does not support the hypothesis of a widespread product reliability problem. A vast majority of users (90.5%) rated their experience as matching their expectations (a 4 or 5 out of 5), suggesting a high degree of product consistency and satisfaction.
Supporting evidence
90.5% of respondents said their product experience matched what

nd satisfaction.
Supporting evidence
90.5% of respondents said their product experience matched what they were looking for (rating it 4 or 5 out of 5).
Only 8.3% of users were neutral or dissatisfied with how well the product matched their expectations (rating it 3 or 2 out of 5).
Similarly, only 1.2% of users rated the 'overall quality of the experience' as the lowest possible score (1 out of 5).
55.7% of budtenders report that they 'Often' or 'Always' stick with and recommend brands they know and trust, suggesting they have found reliable options.

PRESSURE 2
PRESSURE 2 — Awareness is not translating into recommendation
High Brand Awareness Fails to Convert into Unconditional Trust
The one stat: Nearly 80% of all respondents

source: 2026-04-07 - BTA Webinar/BTA Webinar - Initial Data Analysis - 2026-04-07.docx
text:
and Awareness Fails to Convert into Unconditional Trust
The one stat: Nearly 80% of all respondents (79.5%) agree that some cannabis brands promise more than they deliver.
Key finding
While most respondents are very familiar with cannabis brands, this awareness does not automatically lead to trust or loyalty. A significant trust gap exists, driven by a perception that brands overpromise. Trust is ultimately earned through tangible factors like product quality and consistency, not just brand recognition or market presence.
Supporting evidence
A strong majority of respondents (73.4%) report bein

source: 2026-04-07 - BTA Webinar/BTA Webinar - Initial Data Analysis - 2026-04-07.docx
text:
gnition or market presence.
Supporting evidence
A strong majority of respondents (73.4%) report being 'very familiar' with the cannabis brands available in their province.
When asked what builds trust, 'Consistent product quality' was the top factor for nearly all respondents (96.4%), far outpacing factors like 'Strong presence in stores or media' (15.0%).
To increase brand loyalty, the top three drivers cited were 'Consistent product quality across batches' (75.2%), 'Opportunities to sample or test new products' (66.2%), and 'Better education about their products and processes' (65.0%).
Despi

source: 2026-04-07 - BTA Webinar/BTA Webinar - Initial Data Analysis - 2026-04-07.docx
text:
test new products' (66.2%), and 'Better education about their products and processes' (65.0%).
Despite high familiarity, most respondents (66.2%) describe their brand loyalty as only 'somewhat loyal,' stating they have favorites but like to experiment.
What complicates the story
Brand name is still a significant factor in purchase decisions, with a combined 98.7% stating it influences their choice 'somewhat' (72.8%) or 'very strongly' (25.9%).
Advocacy is extremely high once a positive experience is established; 78.6% of respondents are very likely (5/5) to recommend a product they personally

source: 2026-04-07 - BTA Webinar/BTA Webinar - Initial Data Analysis - 2026-04-07.docx
text:
e is established; 78.6% of respondents are very likely (5/5) to recommend a product they personally enjoyed.
Why it matters for the brand
Marketing that focuses only on building brand awareness is insufficient. Without a core focus on product consistency and education, brands risk losing customers at the moment of truth, as familiarity is not enough to secure trust or a final sale.
Quote angle: A respondent describing how they were initially drawn to a brand due to its marketing or packaging, but were let down by inconsistent quality, causing them to now rely more on product specs or budtender

PRESSURE 3
PRESSURE 3 — If the person selling your product isn't confident…
Many budtenders question the effectiveness of their own recommendations
The one stat: Only 57% of budtenders believe that 7 or more out of 10 customers purchase their recommended product.
Key finding
The data indicates a confidence gap, with only a narrow majority of budtenders (57%) feeling their recommendations are highly effective. This suggests a significant porti

source: 2026-04-07 - BTA Webinar/BTA Webinar - Initial Data Analysis - 2026-04-07.docx
text:
dtenders (57%) feeling their recommendations are highly effective. This suggests a significant portion feel less successful, which could be compounded by widespread skepticism towards brand promises, potentially undermining trust at the moment of sale.
Supporting evidence
A significant minority of budtenders (43%) report that 6 or fewer out of every 10 customers purchase their recommendation.
Widespread skepticism exists, with 85% of budtenders agreeing that 'Some brands feel like they promise more than they deliver'.
Lack of training may play a role, as nearly three in ten budtenders (29%) re

source: 2026-04-07 - BTA Webinar/BTA Webinar - Initial Data Analysis - 2026-04-07.docx
text:
ore than they deliver'.
Lack of training may play a role, as nearly three in ten budtenders (29%) report they 'Rarely' or 'Never' receive formal education or training.
When trusting a brand, budtenders rank peer recommendations (56%) significantly lower than their own positive past experiences (79%) or consistent product quality (99%).
What complicates the story
While general effectiveness is questioned, confidence is extremely high when personal experience is involved: 98% of budtenders are likely to recommend a product they personally enjoyed.
Why it matters for the brand

PRESSURE 4
PRESSURE 4 — Your brand is being interpreted at the counter
There is no shared language for describing cannabis brands at retail
The one stat: Descriptions of brand quality are highly personal and subjective

source: 2026-04-07 - BTA Webinar/BTA Webinar - Initial Data Analysis - 2026-04-07.docx
text:
abis brands at retail
The one stat: Descriptions of brand quality are highly personal and subjective, ranging from insider slang ('all hype no gas') to vague praise ('it checks all the boxes').
Key finding
Budtenders, managers, and consumers rely on their own words and experiences to interpret and communicate brand value. The language used is fragmented, varying from specific operational details like 'grow ops' to abstract attributes like 'innovative' and personal feelings like 'has my heart'. This suggests official brand messaging is frequently re-written at the point of sale.

The word 'consistent' or 'consistency' was the mo

text:
UME EDIBLES... NOTHING"
What complicates the story
The word 'consistent' or 'consistency' was the most common descriptor, used over 15 times by all roles to describe a desirable brand attribute, even if what it referred to (e.g., quality, THC, availability) varied.
Why it matters for the brand
This language fragmentation means official brand messaging can be lost at the counter, replaced by individual interpretations. This makes it difficult for brands to build a predictable and uniform identity with consumers.

Monthly Highlights
The perceived value and advocacy for the BTA platform are demonstrating a consistently positive 
trend across various cohorts and regions. The overall platform advocacy NPS (net promoter score for 
the BTA training platform itself) for all cohorts increased 28.8 points in January 2026, reaching a 
strong score of 89.2. This positive momentum has continued into the current quarter, with platform 
advocacy NPS for all cohorts rising moderately by 22.1 points to 86.7 during Jan–Feb QTD. Similarly, 
budtenders’ platfor

cohorts rising moderately by 22.1 points to 86.7 during Jan–Feb QTD. Similarly, 
budtenders’ platform advocacy NPS saw a moderate increase of 26.7 points to 83.9, and for Ontario 
respondents, it increased moderately by 20.3 points to 84.3 during the same QTD period. These recent 
scores consistently surpass the all-time average platform advocacy NPS of 80.3, signalling strong 
engagement with BTA resources.

BTA platform advocacy shows strong, consistent growth across all metrics.
Early signals suggest significant shifts in customer experience feedback and stated loyalty drivers, 
though these require careful interpretation due to small sample sizes. Early signal: Among BC respon­
dents, the proportion mentioning reacting to a negative experience by telling friends or posting online

Methodology & Data Quality
Data Source
This report draws on survey data collected through the BTA (Budtenders Association) platform. 
All respondents are verified, active budtenders enrolled in the BTA training ecosystem. Survey partic­
ipation is opt-in and occurs as part of ongoing training and assessment cycles.
Resp

Survey partic­
ipation is opt-in and occurs as part of ongoing training and assessment cycles.
Respondent Verification
Respondents are authenticated through BTA’s enrolment and session-tracking system. Only respon­
dents with verified budtender status at a licensed retail cannabis location are included in segment-level 
analyses.
Confidence Tiers and N-Thresholds
All metrics and claims in this report are assigned a confidence tier based on the number of qualifying 
respondents (N) in the relevant segment or period:

Comparison Windows
• Quarterly reports: Primary comparison is to the immediately preceding quarter (QoQ). An all-time 
baseline is used for contextual anchoring where available.
• Mon

eceding quarter (QoQ). An all-time 
baseline is used for contextual anchoring where available.
• Monthly reports: Primary comparison is to the prior month (MoM). Quarter-to-date (QTD) aggre­
gates are used where monthly N is insufficient.
• Biannual reports: Spring and Fall waves are compared to the prior equivalent wave (year-over-
year cadence).
Index Construction
Composite indices (e.g., Brand Health Index, Choice Friction Index) are calculated using pre-defined 
question batteries and scoring rubrics maintained in the BTA Question Bank.
Quality Assurance Steps
1. Duplicate response removal

g rubrics maintained in the BTA Question Bank.
Quality Assurance Steps
1. Duplicate response removal: Each respondent-period combination is deduplicated before analy­
sis.
2. Outlier review: Extreme response distributions are flagged for human review before claim gener­
ation.
3. Directional consistency check: Claims where the stated direction contradicts the underlying 
magnitude are automatically flagged as direction_mismatch.
4. Orphan reference check: Claims referencing question IDs not present in the underlying evidence 
packet are flagged as orphan_reference.
5. Human review gate: Any re

ent in the underlying evidence 
packet are flagged as orphan_reference.
5. Human review gate: Any report with a readiness score below 0.70 or with unresolved directional 
mismatch flags requires analyst sign-off before distribution.
Limitations
• This report reflects the perceptions and behaviours of BTA-enrolled budtenders and does not repre­
sent the broader cannabis retail workforce.
• Response rates vary by segment and period; lower-N periods may reflect seasonal variation or 
enrolment fluctuations rather than true attitude shifts.
• All findings are observational. No causal inferences sh

tuations rather than true attitude shifts.
• All findings are observational. No causal inferences should be drawn from this data.

emerging trend indicating a re-evaluation of loyalty programs and rewards as primary drivers for 
store returns, alongside a reduced propensity for consumers to publicly share negative experiences.

reflecting a generally positive operational environment. Platform Advocacy NPS (Net Promoter Score 
for the platform), a measure of user willingness to recommend, sits strong at 80.3, underpinned by 
an overall Experience Quality Index (a measure of overall positive perception of the retail experience)

ential incentives such as product samples (91.5%), personal interest in topics (66.0%), and a desire to 
see the impact of their input (64.2%).

Implications & Looking Ahead
The Canadian regulated cannabis market is marked by strong consumer brand loyalty, with over half of 
consumers consistently returning to a few ‘go-to’ brands. While nearly four in ten consumers engage 
in some level of brand exploration, outright disregard for brands is rare. This suggests brands benefit 
from established preferences but also have an opportunity to attract consumers through differentiated 
offerings rather than solely relying on novelty.
Despite a strong overall consumer experience qua

retail sector and consumers largely approaching purchases with moderate planning, opportunities 
exist to foster deeper engagement and confidence. Brand Trust Index (66.4) and Choice Friction Index 
(56.4) are competitive but indicate potential for improvement. The relatively low incidence of ‘Calm’, 
‘Curious’, or ‘Confident’ purchase states, coupled with a notably weaker overall consumer advocacy 
Net Promoter Score of 34.0, suggests that positi

minimal driver for purchase decisions.

fundamental challenges in brand visibility and advocacy. The brand needs to implement targeted 
initiatives to elevate Top-of-Mind awareness (currently 1.6%) and convert its high familiarity (96.8%)
